topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every carefully chosen anecdote, every carefully phrased triumph, was a stranger’s voice. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary gasp that echoed in the sterile silence of my apartment. I stared at the dates, the milestones, the triumphs that were undeniably mine, yet they were narrated by someone else. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, turning my skin tight. I crumpled the page, the sound a violent rip in the quiet room. My hands trembled so badly I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady them. "This is a fabrication," I whispered, the sound raspy and unfamiliar. I paced the length of the room, the floorboards protesting beneath my frantic strides. Every shadow seemed to coalesce into a mocking face.

[story 2]

I reread the text three times, my eyes darting back and forth across the meticulously crafted narrative. A cold, hard knot tightened in my stomach, radiating outward like a poison. The ink seemed to writhe before my eyes. When I finally looked up, the room spun slightly. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I slammed the file shut, the sound sharp and final. I stood up abruptly, knocking over a stack of books. The resulting clatter was swallowed by the sudden, roaring emptiness inside me. I began to pace, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, my gaze fixed on the ceiling as if searching for an escape route from the suffocating reality unfolding in my mind.

[story 3]

The sheer audacity of it hit me like a physical blow. I sat down heavily on the edge of the sofa, the springs groaning in protest. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the coffee table, the pressure building until my vision blurred at the edges. I let out a low, guttural sound, half-choke, half-snarl, and squeezed my eyes shut, trying to physically push the images away. When I opened them again, the room seemed too bright, the colors too harsh. I stood up, my movements stiff, deliberate. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, my fists clenched so tightly my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. The air felt thick, heavy, and utterly unbearable.
